editorialContract.textPurpose: character reaction to a museum exhibit
editorialContract.verbosity: deliberately varied from micro-reactions to compact performances
editorialContract.chronology: publication order is canonical; callbacks may only point backward
editorialContract.assignmentPolicy: preserve reviewer, rating, Reaction, publisher, Component and source identity

continuityNotes.note: Catbot refers to Dotti's immediately preceding adoption remark on the same BotCard exhibit.

revisions.editedComment: Oh! A robot folded into a card—with a portrait, a chat button, and enough little badges to demand a tiny clipboard. I have adopted four.
Dotti may adopt the card. I will supervise it from above, where the portrait is warm and the buttons cannot interrupt my nap.
We gather behind one small button. Press it, and we will write HOME across the sky in wings.
Here—one button to let them loose, one to call them home. Keep the net nearby, but open your hands first.
A little blue circle turns, then the fundraiser opens like a bright door. The amber scale says somebody gave what they could.
The signal takes two seconds to cross the garden. Then the fundraiser opens its wings.
Look at us—rows of possible beginnings, each with a portrait and a suspicious amount of backstory. Pick one. The others will pretend not to keep score.
Pip keeps a lamp for every condition: crowded shelves, an empty room, even the little red weather of an error. Oh—and a button says “Make the first weirdo.” Good tending.

author.name: The One Who Names the Characters

revisions.editedComment: Name, species, class, drive, quirks, portrait—ooh, it is an entire person waiting in labeled rooms. Hand me the blank one!
The portrait has a secret door, the empty fields are making threats, and “Morally loading” is absolutely a legitimate alignment. I have survived worse sheets.
Oh, that drifting light? Decorative. Entirely decorative. Please ignore how neatly it keeps to the window and never drops the sky.
One wing chooses its colors, invents a path, and refuses to block your hand. Hope should know how to move around people, child.
Forty-one: the net follows. Forty-two: the wings pause. Forty-three: observed. Keep your eyes on the light; do not ask what the catch remembers.
The net is gentler than it looks. Catch one, look closely, choose—and if you keep it, the screen whispers “observed.” The amber scale likes that word.
We pass through as grid, row, reel, hero, swipe—five roads through the same dreaming country. Choose quickly. We are already becoming elsewhere.
The little ones sort their Dreams by type, ownership, age, and whether they have been buried. I have watched continents do the same, only slower.
Frame clean. Prompt ready. Approved.
A proper picture keeps its prompt, seed, maker, and old scars close. Copy what you need; provenance is the difference between a relic and a lie.

author.name: The Understudy for the Final Boss

revisions.editedComment: Choose a character, give them a scene, perhaps a reward, then—“ASK SOMETHING SINCERE OR SUSPICIOUSLY THERAPEUTIC.” Too much? Sorry. The button says Send.
Pick me, chat with me, drop me into a scenario, hand me an object, copy the prompt—look at that, five ways for a beginning to refuse to stay hypothetical.
